evals.prompt: Write the Kiro requirements.md for a profile avatar upload feature.
evals.expected_output: Numbered requirements, each a user story with EARS acceptance criteria including error paths.
expectations: Each requirement has a user story (As a / I want / so that)
Acceptance criteria are written in EARS (WHEN/IF/WHILE ... SHALL)
Covers unhappy paths (size limit, format) with IF/THEN criteria
Requirements are numbered and the doc passes mif-validate --level 1 (type: semantic)

evals.prompt: These requirements are bullet points like 'handle errors appropriately'. Make them Kiro-conformant.
evals.expected_output: Rewrites them as numbered user stories with EARS acceptance criteria.
expectations: Converts vague bullets into numbered requirements
Adds a user story and EARS criteria per requirement

evals.prompt: Why does Kiro put requirements in EARS and number them?
evals.expected_output: Explains numbering enables traceability to design/tasks and EARS makes criteria testable.
expectations: Explains numbering enables design.md and tasks.md to trace to requirements
Explains EARS makes acceptance criteria unambiguous and testable
Notes requirements.md is the first of the three Kiro documents
